coredata.dc:title: Chromium speciation by isophthalic acid-doped polymer dots as sensitive and selective fluorescent probes
coredata.dc:creator: Zare-Moghadam, Mousa
Shamsipur, Mojtaba
Molaabasi, Fatemeh
Hajipour-Verdom, Behnam
coredata.dc:description: Hexavalent chromium is a known carcinogen, among all species of chromium ions, for the respiratory tract in humans. In the present work, a new facile probe is developed for rapid and sensitive determination of Cr(VI) based on utilizing highly fluorescent conjugated poly[(9,9-dioctylfluorenyl-2,7-diyl)-alt-co-(1,4-benzo-(2,1’,3) thiadiazole)] (PFBT) polymer dots (PDs). The PDs are easily functionalized by doping of isophthalic acid (IPA) into the target PDs during a single step preparation. The prepared PDs with an average diameter of 30 nm illustrated a strong fluorescence with an emission peak centered at 530 nm (photo-excited at 480 nm). The strong fluorescence of PDs is selectively and significantly quench with Cr(VI), while it does not change by Cr(III) ion and, thus, can facilitate a chromium speciation process. The proposed mechanism is an inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism, in which the absorption bands of Cr(IV) overlaps with the emission and excitation bands of the modified PDs. The prepared PDs revealed a good linear relationship from 0.1 to 1000 μmol L−1 for Cr(VI) with a detection limit of 0.03 μmol L−1, which further used to track the Cr distribution in water samples. Finally, the IPA-doped PDs with excellent optical properties, biocompatibility, and high quantum yield showed promising potential in tracking Cr species and specifying of different Cr ions inside the human cells, which opening a new door toward getting a better insight into the cell function and metabolism in the presence of heavy metal ions, and especially chromium ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Speciation
Chromium
Inner filter effect
Polymer dots
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of IPA doped PFBT PDs
Fluorescence response of PDs to Cr(VI)
Application to real water samples and validity test by reference materials
Applicability of PDs for tracking of Cr(Ⅵ) in bioimaging

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The financial support of this work Iran Science Elites Federation (ISEF) and Research Councils of Tarbiat Modares and Razi Universities is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorption (black), excitation (green), and emission (red) spectra of PFBT PDs. (b) TEM image of IPA doped PDs. (c) Hydrodynamic diameters of pure PDs measured by DLS. (d) Hydrodynamic diameters of IPA doped PDs. (e) Zeta potential of PDs (red line), PDs-Cr(VI) mixture (green line). (f) IR spectrum of pure PDs (purple) and IPA doped PDs (red). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) PL spectra of PDs at different excitation wavelengths. (b) Photostability of PDs. (c) Fluorescence intensity of PDs at different pHs. (d) Fluorescence intensity of PDs in the presence of analyte at different pHs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of IPA doped PDs in aqueous solution upon an increase in Cr(VI) ion concentration from 0 to 979.9 μM. (b) Stern Volmer plot of IPA doped PDs quenched by increasing concentration of Cr(VI) ion in aqueous solution (red) and corresponding calibration curve for Cr(III) ion (green). (c) Fluorescence response of PDs in the presence of Cr(VI) and Cr(III) mixture before (red) and after (green) transforming of Cr(III) to Cr(VI) in the presence of hydrogen peroxide. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Hydrodynamic diameters of IPA doped PDs (green plot) and AIP doped PDs in presence of Cr(VI) (red plot) (b) Absorption peaks of Cr(VI) and PDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Fluorescence response of PDs in the presence of different cations.
Bright field and fluorescence imaging of Hela cells stained with PDs and stained with PDs/Cr(III) and PDs/Cr(VI).
Schematic illustration of preparation of PDs and their application to chromium speciation.
Comparison of the analytical performance of different probes for detection of chromium species.

row.entry: Cr(VI) and Cr(III) by oxidation of Cr(III) Inner filter effect
Cr(VI) and Cr(III) by oxidation of Cr(III) and static quenching
Cr(VI) dynamic quenching and inner filter effect

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Application of PDs for the determination of Cr(VI) ion in real samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The results were expressed as the mean of triplicate samples ± standard error (SE).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Validation of PDs by reference materials.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The results were expressed as the mean of triplicate samples ± standard error (SE).

head.ce:title: Chromium speciation by isophthalic acid-doped polymer dots as sensitive and selective fluorescent probes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biomaterials and Tissue Engineering, Breast Cancer Research Center, Motamed Cancer Institute, ACECR, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biomaterials and Tissue Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biomaterials and Tissue Engineering, Breast Cancer Research Center, Motamed Cancer Institute, ACECR, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biophysics, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, 14115-154, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biophysics, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, 14115-154, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hexavalent chromium is a known carcinogen, among all species of chromium ions, for the respiratory tract in humans. In the present work, a new facile probe is developed for rapid and sensitive determination of Cr(VI) based on utilizing highly fluorescent conjugated poly[(9,9-dioctylfluorenyl-2,7-diyl)-alt-co-(1,4-benzo-(2,1’,3) thiadiazole)] (PFBT) polymer dots (PDs).

The PDs are easily functionalized by doping of isophthalic acid (IPA) into the target PDs during a single step preparation. The prepared PDs with an average diameter of 30 nm illustrated a strong fluorescence with an emission peak centered at 530 nm (photo-excited at 480 nm). The strong fluorescence of PDs is selectively and significantly quench with Cr(VI), while it does not change by Cr(III) ion and, thus, can facilitate a chromium speciation process. The proposed mechanism is an inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism, in which the absorption bands of Cr(IV) overlaps with the emission and excitation bands of the modified PDs. The prepared PDs revealed a good linear relationship from 0.1 to 1000 μmol L−1 for Cr(VI) with a detection limit of 0.03 μmol L−1, which further used to track the Cr distribution in water samples. Finally, the IPA-doped PDs with excellent optical properties, biocompatibility, and high quantum yield showed promising potential in tracking Cr species and specifying of different Cr ions inside the human cells, which opening a new door toward getting a better insight into the cell function and metabolism in the presence of heavy metal ions, and especially chromium ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal species with great differences in their chemical properties and biological operations, originated from a variety of industrial activities, human wastes and acidic rain, can seriously pollute the river and ground water by digestion of soil [1–3]. Among these, chromium (Cr) as the chromium (Cr) as the seventh abundant metal, is of vital importance [4]. In the past decades, the increased usage of chromium for industrial purposes has resulted in an increased amount of chromium species in the biosphere. Hence, chromium is well-known as an essential contaminant of environment. In environmental matrices, Cr mainly exists in two oxidation states of hexavalent [Cr(VI)] and trivalent [Cr(III)]. However, while Cr(VI) is a harmful carcinogenic species, even at meager amounts, Cr(III) is known as a naturally occurring micronutrient necessary for the human body [4]. Thus, chromium speciation is of critical importance to ensure the safety of water and food for human being. On the other hand, by the application of commonly used analytical instrumental methods such as atomic absorption spectrometry it is not only impossible to differentiate between each chromium species, but also is almost impossible to detect total amount of chromium, due to its presence at low levels, in one hand, and also the high complexity of the environmental and biological matrices, in the other. Thus, in many cases, a pre-concentration step is necessary to improve the limit of detection and selectivity of such analytical methods. In fact, the analysis of food and environmental and biological samples for chromium content requires a reliable separation and subsequent quantification of both Cr(VI) and Cr(III) species [5,6].

Chromium has several oxidation states, among which Cr(VI) and Cr(III) are chemically stable and usually found in the environmental system [7,8]. Cr(III) as one of the essential elements of the body has a significant role in the biological behavior of enzymes, proteins, and nucleic acids, so that they can have their natural activities. Also, it has also an important function in the metabolism of lipids, proteins, and carbohydrates [8]. On the contrary, Cr(VI) is known as a strong oxidizing agent in an environment with so small size that can be simply penetrates to cells [9]. Cr(VI) is one hundred times life-threatening than Cr(III), owing to its carcinogenic effects. Meanwhile, Cr(VI) possesses the highest mobility among all oxidation states of Cr and, therefore, has the highest toxicity over all of them [10], so that several million workers via inhalation and skin contact have been worldwide exposed to Cr(VI). The World Health Organization (WHO) has set an upper concentration limit of Cr(VI) in natural drinking water as 0.05 mg L−1 [11]. Thus, it is essential to determine the selectively and sensitively of chromium species in environment and in waste of industrial activities such as electroplating, pigment and cement production of pigment, leather tanning, wood preservation and metal plating [12].

Several analytical techniques that commonly used for chromium detection include UV–visible spectrophotometry [13], atomic absorption spectrometry [14,15], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry [16], and electrochemical techniques [17]. However, although these techniques are highly sensitive, many of them suffer from such drawbacks as high cost, troublous pretreatment procedure, long operation time, and complicated instruments, which restrict their broad applications. For instance, by atomic absorption spectrometry it is impossible to differentiate between each chromium species [14,15]. Moreover, due to low levels of chromium and also the high complexity of the environmental and biological matrices, a pre-concentration step such as solid phase extraction of the Cr(VI)- Dowex M 4195 chelating resin [14] or that of Cr(VI)–APDC chelate on the Ambersorb 563 carbonaceous resin filled mini-column [15] is necessary to improve the limit of detection and selectivity of atomic absorption spectrometry as the analytical method. Meanwhile, in the case of analysis of food, the environmental and biological samples for chromium content with graphite furnace atomic absorption spectrometry, it is required to carry out a reliable separation process, which needs organic solvents and ligands that may pollute the environment [17]. Most importantly, the above-mentioned methods are not able to track chromium species in living cells and tissues. Thus, in the past decade, the wide application of a range of nano-materials has opened a new era in chromium speciation and/or highly selective and sensitive detection of Cr(VI) in environmental samples [18].

Very recently, a range of highly fluorescent nano-materials has been synthesized, characterized, and applied as suitable probes to the detection of chromium in environmental and biological samples [19–30]. Some of these include a variety of carbon dots [19–21], graphene quantum dots [22], silver nanoclusters [23], silica nanoparticles [24], graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets [25], silicon quantum dots [26] and iridium nanoclusters [27] have attracted an increasing interest for recognition of chromium(VI) in water samples. However, thus far, none of them have been applied to the detection of chromium ions in living cells. Also, carbon dots need a high temperature and pressure under hydrothermal condition [31] and other are multi step in nature [22], which can result in a time-consuming method [19]. Notably, the speciation analysis of Cr(III) and Cr(VI) has not been studied in most cases [32,33].

Semiconducting polymer dots (PDs), consisting of π-conjugated polymers, are well known as highly fluorescent nanoprobes of high quantum yield and possessing excellent photo-stability and biocompatibility [30,34]. Thus, they have received an increasing attentions of scientists for broad use in single molecule tracking, photothermal therapy [35,36], photoacoustic imaging [28], pH sensing [37], metal ion detection [38–42], bio-sensing [43–47], and etc. Furthermore, recent advances in the exterior functionalization of PDs have converted them to good candidates for in-vivo and in-vitro bioimaging, owing to their low toxicity, notable biocompatibility and convenient surface modification [42,46,48–50].

We have recently prepared and characterized poly[(9,9-dioctylfluorenyl-2,7-diyl)-co-(1,4-benzo-thiadiazole)] (PFBT) dots modified with 9-anthracencene carboxylic acid and Nile red. These PDs were then successfully applied to the highly sensitive and selective FRET-based detection of aflatoxin B1 [44], and were used as “off–on” fluorescent probes for iron, copper and histidine detection and imaging in living cells [42] and, as the hydrophobicity probes, to follow the protein conformational changes and plasma membrane imaging [46]. However, to the best of our knowledge, there are no previous reports on the application of PDs for speciation of chromium and sensitive detection of Cr(VI). Thus, in this work, for the first time, we wish to report on the successful use of conjugated PFBT PDs for the purposes mentioned above.

In this study, we developed a new simple production of PDs surfaces functionalized by carboxyl groups, via doping of IPA within the fluorescent conjugated polymer PFBT, so that the –COOH functional groups were positioned outside of the PDs. This approach was a one-step procedure by which the designed PDs-based fluorescence probe was prepared in less than 1 h. The probe was then used as an IFE-based fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective detection of Cr(VI) by a considerable fluorescence quenching. Moreover, by using the prepared PDs, we demonstrated an interesting chromium speciation method after oxidation of Cr(III) to Cr(VI) using hydrogen peroxide, so that we could determine the total chromium present and, consequently, the amount of trivalent chromium (Cr(III)) was evaluated from the difference between total chromium and Cr(VI) concentrations. The synthetic procedure and application of the functionalized PDs in speciation are illustrated in Scheme 1 .

It should be noted that Cr(VI) can easily cross the cellular membrane via a sulfate transport pathway and reduce to reactive intermediates Cr(V), Cr(IV) and Cr(III). This is then followed by producing reactive oxygen species (ROS), which then cause breaking of the DNA strand, lipid peroxidation, and base modifications [49,51]. Thus, the intracellular monitoring of chromium states in living cells is of vital importance, due to the cytotoxicity, apoptotic signaling mechanism, oxidative stress, and physiological function of cells; however, there is a limited data available in this regard. Thus, in this work, we successfully investigated the performance of the designed PDs for chromium distribution inside the cells [50,52].

All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Poly[(9,9-dioctylfluorenyl-2,7-diyl)-alt-co-(1,4-benzo-(2,1’,3)-thiadiazole)] (PFBT, MW of 10 kDa, and polydispersity index (PDI) of 2.3) NaCl, NaOH, Cu(NO3)2, NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4 were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Isophthalic acid (IPA) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, Missouri, USA). Reagent grade K2Cr2O7, KCl, NaCl, NaOH, BaCl2, CaCl2, MgCl2, FeCl2, HgCl2, MnCl2, NiCl2, SrCl2, ZrCl2, AgNO3, SnCl2, AlCl3, Co(NO3)2, Cu(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2, FeCl3, Ce(NO3)3, Cr(NO3)3 were obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Doubly distilled deionized water was used throughout the experiments. The Seronorm Trace Elements Urine reference was obtained from Sero (Billingstad, Norway).

The UV–vis spectra were recorded using a Scinco UV S-2100 (Cinco, Korea), spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence measurements were obtained on a PerkinElmer LS-50B fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer, U.K.) with a xenon-lamp light source. The emission spectra were recorded over the wavelength range of 400–700 nm upon excitation at 480 nm at a scan rate of 1500 nm min−1. The spectral band passes for excitation and emission were set at 10 nm. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken using a Tecnai G2 F20 (Philips, Holland) operating at 150 KV. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential were measured using a Zetasizer nano ZS series dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Malvern, U.K.). The fluorescence images were obtained using a Nikon Eclipse TE2000-S apparatus (Nikon Instruments, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of IPA doped PFBT PDs
ce:section.ce:para: Our strategy for functionalizing the surface of PDs was based on co-precipitation driven by hydrophobic interactions during nanoparticle formation. IPA was doped within PFBT in aqueous solution by using the nanoprecipitation method [36]. Accordingly, 50 μL of PFBT solution (1 mg mL−1 in THF) and 10 μL of IPA solution (1 mg mL−1 in water) were added to the 2.5 mL of THF, and the mixture was sonicated to form a homogeneous solution. Then, the solution was quickly injected into 5 mL of water under vigorous shaking and sonication for 2–3 min. During nanoparticle formation, the hydrophobic part of IPA was most likely embedded inside the PDs, while the hydrophilic functional carboxyl groups extended out into the aqueous environment. Under the protection of N2 gas, the solution was heated on a 90 °C hot plate to evaporate the THF solvent. The modified PDs solution was filtered through a 0.2 μm cellulose membrane filter.

For sensing of Cr(VI), 20 μL of PDs and 2.0 mL of citrate buffer solution (10 mM, pH 5) were mixed and transferred to a 1.0 cm quartz cuvette. The fluorescence spectra were recorded the wavelength over the range of 500–700 nm under an excitation wavelength of 480 nm. The excitation and emission slits were kept at 10 nm. Different concentrations of Cr(VI) were successively added to a cuvette to reach final concentrations of 0.25 μM–1000 μM, and after 1 min, the fluorescence spectra were recorded at room temperature. In speciation procedure, at first Cr(VI) and Cr(III) were mixed and added to PDs solution to record the change in fluorescence spectrum. Then, for total chromium determination, the Cr(III) content was fully oxidized to Cr(VI), using 30% hydrogen peroxide (50-times stoichiometric excess) at room temperature in basic pH (i.e., pH > 10). The transformed mixture solution was shaken for 15 min and then the excess H2O2 was eliminated by thermal decomposition. Finally, the total concentration of Cr(VI) was determined [48].

The human cervical cancer (HeLa) cells obtained from National Cell Bank of Iran (NCBI) at the Pasteur Institute of Iran were used in this study. The HeLa cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) in a neutral pH (7.2–7.4) that supplemented with 15% (v/v) heat-inactivated (50 °C, 30 min) FBS, 2 mM glutamine, 100 units mL−1 of penicillin and 100 mg mL−1 of streptomycin at 37 °C, and 5% CO2 in a humidified incubator. Then, the cells were washed with 1X phosphate saline buffer (PBS) and trypsinized (0.025% trypsin, 0.02% EDTA) after they were reached until 70–90% confluent. After attaching overnight, the cells were used in fluorescence imaging experiments. Briefly, after washing the cells with PBS, they were separately treated with 50 μM Cr(III) and Cr(VI) ions and incubated for 2 h at 37 °C. Then, the cells were washed twice, and incubated with DMEM containing 10 μM (μL mL−1) of COOH- functionalized PFBT for 2 h at 37 °C. After that, the cells were washed three times to remove extracellular free probes, and at the end, 1 mL DMEM was added to the cells medium, before observation by a fluorescence microscope (Nikon Eclipse 80i).

In this work, we performed a simple method for functionalization of the PDs, via the addition of IPA as a hydrophobic agent, into the final polymer formation step. In order to comprehend our procedure for sensing of chromium ion, we rectified the conjugated PDs by co-nanoprecipitation, based on a previously described method [28]. Briefly, the modified PFBT dots were prepared using a pre-solution mixture with a constant PFBT concentration and an IPA/PFBT portion limit of 20 wt %. Our method for functionalizing the outside of PDs was based on trapping of IPA into a single PD driven by hydrophobic forces during conjugated polymer dots formation. A small amount of IPA is sufficient for co-precipitation with the PFBT polymers to modify and functionalize the PDs surface.

In order to characterization of the synthesized PDs, the optical property, morphology, and structure of PDs were evaluated by UV–vis, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS) and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). The related results are shown in Fig. 1 . Fig. 1a shows the UV–vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of the prepared PDs. As seen, the excitation and emission wavelengths for IPA/PFBT PDs are 480 nm and 530 nm, respectively. The TEM image shown in Fig. 1b well exhibited that the size of PDs is around 20 nm (Fig. 1b). We also estimated the hydrodynamic size and surface potentials of the PDs by dynamic light scattering (DLS) method. DLS measurements showed an average diameter of 28 nm and zeta potential of −13 mV for pure PDs (see Fig. 1c and e). The observed growth in size obtained by DLS, in comparison to that by TEM is referred to the hydrodynamic radius of the as-prepared PDs [9]. On the other hand, the particle diameter and zeta potential of the IPA doped PDs were recorded as 36 nm and −27.5 mV, respectively (Fig. 1d and e). As is obvious, compared to the non-functionalized PDs, the IPA-doped PDs demonstrated a minimal increase of about 8 nm in the hydrodynamic size and more negative zeta potential, from −13 mV to −27.5 mV, which nicely proved the surface functionalization of the PFBT PDs by IPA.

For characterization of the surface of functionalized PDs, the FT-IR spectra were recorded, and the results are shown in Fig. 1f. As seen, there are many peaks which proved the fact the procedure of synthesis is correctly performed. These important peaks are as follows: The broad peak at 3400 cm−1 assigned to stretching vibration of the O–H group. The peaks at 2853 and 2923 cm−1 are characteristic of aliphatic and aromatic C–H groups, respectively. The peak at 2360 cm−1 may be attributed to a combination of CN and CC. The peak at 1627 cm−1 assigned to CO group, due to the presence of IPA and the peak at 1257 cm−1, due to the coupling of (COH) and (CO) from functional groups of IPA present at the surface of the modified PDs.

By scanning of the IPA doped PDs, the 480 nm and 530 nm were obtained as the best excitation and emission wavelengths, respectively (see Fig. 2 a). As seen, no shift was occurred during the wavelength scanning, well-indicated that the PDs are monodisperse to a large extent. Photostability of PDs is the main concern in many of their applications, especially in biosensing and long-term cellular imaging [21]. For this reason, the PDs excited at 480 nm and their fluorescence emission, centered at 530 nm, were recorded in the course of a 90 min duration, measured in a time period of 5 min, and the resulting signal intensity versus time plot is shown in Fig. 2b. As seen, the PDs revealed relatively good photostability for 90 min, so that they can be used for fluorescence sensing and imaging purposes.

In the next step, the influence of solution pH, over the range of 3.0–9.0, on the fluorescence intensity of the developed PDs was investigated and the results are shown in Fig. 2c. As seen, the probe exhibited a maximum fluorescence intensity at pH 5. On the other hand, the effect of solution pH on Cr(VI) ion sensing was also investigated over the same range of 3.0–9.0 and the results are shown in Fig. 2d. As clearly seen, the presence of Cr(VI) also exhibited the largest decrease in relative intensity at a pH 5.0. The possible explanation for this observation at various pHs could be attributed to the carboxyl group on the PDs due to the positively charged IPA at pH 5, which make the IPA doped PDs prone to interaction with the negatively charged Cr(VI) anions in solution. In other words, at higher pHs, the sensitivity was decreased, because of the repulsion of the negative charges on both the PDs and Cr(VI) anions. In this way, the zeta potential of IPA-doped PDs was obtained as −27.5 mV at pH 7 and -3 mV at pH 5 because of the surface neutralization, which further supports the proposed reason for the observed effect of the solution pH.

Since the incubation time has also a great influence on the fluorescence results, in the next step, we investigated the time necessary for the IPA doped PDs to reach a constant fluorescence for a Cr(VI) solution. For this purpose, we added a 200 μM solution of Cr(VI) to a proper probe solution, under optimized experimental conditions, and measured the change in its fluorescence intensity by several 30 s intervals. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of the probe was increased by increasing the incubation time up to 60 s and remained constant at longer incubation times. Thus, a 1 min incubation time was selected. for further studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of PDs to Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: To test the feasibility of the designed probe for Cr(VI) detection, under the optimum conditions, we prepared different concentrations ranging from 0 to 1000 μM from a stock solution of potassium dichromate ion followed by introducing them to 20 μL of the developed PDs. After that, the mixture was diluted to 2 mL by citrate buffer of pH 5 and transferred to a 1.0 cm quartz cuvette. Then, after 1 min of incubation time, the fluorescence emission spectra, over the range of 480–700 nm, were collected under excitation at 480 nm at room temperature, while the excitation and emission slits were both set at 10 nm. As it is seen from Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence response of the IPA functionalized PFBT PDs, as a novel fluorescent sensor, was effectively quenched in the presence of Cr(VI) ions (over the concentration range of 0.25, 4.9, 20.0, 34.9, 59.9, 89.9, 129.9, 179.9, 229.9, 329.9, 579.9, 979.9 μM). In order to determine the figures of merit for the fluorescent probe used, the calibration curve for Cr(VI) was acquired by using a various concentration of standard analyte solution from 1 to 1000 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.9932 (Fig. 3b). The detection limit was then calculated as 0.03 μM by using 3 S b/m. Here, the Sb value was calculated from the standard deviation obtained from the mean value from analyzing 10 blank solutions divided by the maximum fluorescence intensity F0 of the blank signals. The evaluated Sb value was finally divided by the slop of the corresponding calibration curve to obtain the detection limit. The results thus obtained clearly proved that the IPA/PFBT PDs is an excellent probe for use in water samples based on EPA and WHO recommendations [37]. Moreover, the calibration curve was also recorded for Cr(III) ion and also included in Fig. 3b for comparison with that for Cr(VI), which provided the feasibility studies for the speciation of chromium ions.

Table 1 compares the figures of merit of IPA/PFBT PDs probe with those of several previously published nanomaterial-based fluorescent sensors for speciation and detection of chromium species [19–21,23,26,27,53,54]. The table shows that, except with a silver nanocluster probe, not only the applicability of the designed probe for the speciation analysis of Cr(III) and Cr(VI) are obviously demonstrated among that of the most probes, but also from the viewpoints of sensitivity, selectivity and dynamic linear range the designed probe possesses some improvements over most of previous papers. The novel isophthalic acid-doped PDs probe is also prepared as one-step less than 1 h, and thus it is relatively cheap and flexible without any complicated chemical modification. Compared to the fluorescent organic dye and quantum dots, the functionalized PDs are much more photostable and biocompatible, characteristics that widen their potential bioapplications in different fields.

Since the speciation of chromium ion for identifying properly between trivalent chromium (Cr(III)) and hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) has become an important research topic, the IPA-doped PDs were tested to meet this goal, considering the specific response of the designed PDs to Cr(VI). In the first step, the PDs were applied to the assessing of amount of Cr(VI) in the mixture solution of chromium. After that, the transformation of chromium was occurred by oxidizing of Cr(III) to Cr(VI) with 30% hydrogen peroxide in alkali solution, upon heating for 40 min at 80 °C [38]. As it is seen from Fig. 3c, at first, minimal quenching (red line) occurs in the presence of 34 μM mixture of Cr(VI) and Cr(III), because only Cr(VI) can quench the fluorescence of PDs. However, after complete transformation of Cr(III) to Cr(VI) under the procedure mentioned above, the amount of signal quenching (green line) was doubled, so that recoveries of 97% (±3.2) and 99% (±4.5) were obtained by three replicates before and after transformation of chromium species, respectively. These results indicated that the IPA-doped PDs are very efficient to identifying the Cr(VI) and Cr(III)species via a simple procedure.

Fluorescence quenching of the designed PDs could be attributed to such different causes as static and dynamic quenching, FRET and inner filter effect (IFE). Fig. 4 a shows the hydrodynamic diameters of IPA doped PDs in the absence (green plot) and presence of Cr(VI) species (red plot). The insignificant change observed in the diameter of IPA-doped PDs particles in the absence (39 nm) and presence (45 nm) of Cr(VI) indicated that the aggregation of PDs does not occur during fluorescence quenching and, thus, the IFE can be offered as the primary quenching mechanism. Fig. 4b shows the absorption peaks of Cr(VI) and PDs. Meanwhile, for the occurrence of IFE mechanism to occur, it is essential to have significant spectral overlap among the absorption band of the analyte and the excitation and emission spectral bands of the fluorescent chemical compound. As it is seen from Fig. 4b, Cr(VI) ion has broad absorption peaks at 300 and 360 nm, while the IPA-doped PFBT PDs possess two broad absorption bands centered at 330 and 480 nm, respectively. As is quite evident, there is an effective overlapping between the absorption signals of Cr(VI) and PDs. This matches with the excitation peak of PDs (~480 nm) which produces an emission peak at 530 nm. Accordingly, the strong overlapping of the chromium and PDs absorption spectra, could result in occurrence of a pronounced inner filter effect, which in turn leads to a significant decrease in fluorescence signal of PDs at 530 nm [1,25].

To evaluate the fluorescence sensing of the surface functionalized PDs, the fluorescence signal of the PDs were examined in the presence of sundry metal ions, such as Hg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Ce2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Fe3+, K+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+ at the concentrations of 100 μM (see Fig. 5 ). As clearly seen from Fig. 5, the PL intensity of the PDs was significantly quenched in the presence of Cr2O7 2− ions, while its fluorescence intensity was minimally changed by other metal ions. The good selectivity of the PDs towards Cr2O7 2− can be related to the presence of isophthalic acid molecules in PDS, that created carboxylate groups on the surface of PDs with ability of Cr(VI) ion interaction by electrostatic forces. On the other hand, for an explanation of good selectivity of PDs to Cr2O7 2−, the effects of IFE and pH of the solution should be considered. It is well known that a variety of specialized treatment processes for the removal of heavy metals prior to their discharge into the environment have been developed. Of all the treatment techniques, heavy metal hydroxide precipitation is the most commonly employed method because of its low cost and simplicity. This process is as simply achieved by increasing the pH of the solution using lime (CaO) or caustic soda (NaOH) to precipitate and, hence, immobilize them as their respective hydroxides. In fact, the solubility product of the interfering heavy ions in this work are in the range of 1.0 × 10−32 for Al3+ ion to 2.2 × 10−14 for Cd2+ ion [55]. Thus, increasing the pH of test solution by 2–3 units is expected to significantly remove the interference effect of most of the interfering ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application to real water samples and validity test by reference materials
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the applicability use of the PDs, the standard addition method was tested for the determination of Cr(VI) ions in real samples such as river water and tap water. At first, the samples were filtered through a 0.45 μm pore size membrane to eliminate the large particles, and after that, the samples were spiked with two concentrations of 10 and 30 μM, respectively. The resulting recoveries of Cr(VI) have shown in Table 2 . According to the obtained results, recoveries were achieved in the range of 98.7–99.7%, confirming the feasibility and reliability of the developed PDs-based probe for chromium ion assay in water samples.

To verify the applicability and validity of the proposed procedure to real samples, the determination of Cr(IV) in a Certified Reference Material was investigated by our designed IPA doped PFBT PDs fluorescent probe. Thus, to the lyophilized Seronorm Trace Elements Urine was added exactly 5 mL of pure water and let to stand for 30 min, and then transferred to a plastic tube [56]. Finally, after appropriate dilution of the standard and sample with Milli-Q ultrapure water, the general procedure given in section 2.4. was carefully followed and their Cr(VI) content were evaluated using the prepared IPA doped PFBT PDs. The results expressed.

As the mean of triplicate samples and corresponding standard error (SE), in μM, are given in Table 3 , while a p-value of <0.05 was considered statistically significant. As is obvious, Table 3 clearly revealed a satisfactory agreement between the analyzed and certified values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applicability of PDs for tracking of Cr(Ⅵ) in bioimaging
ce:section.ce:para: Tracking heavy metals such as chromium in the biological environment and human body are of great attention due to its association with cytotoxicity, oxidative stress, and carcinogenicity [57]. Because of high selectivity and photostability of isophthalic acid-doped PDs, in this study, we applied them for fluorescence imaging of the human cervical cancer cells (HeLa cells). For this reason, HeLa cells were incubated with 15 μL mL−1 of PDs and as seen in Fig. 6 , the compatibility of PDs was successfully demonstrated due to maintaining cell morphology as well as a good response to the fluorescence imaging. As distinctly seen from Fig. 6, the fluorescence intensity in the presence of Cr(III) was not quenched and even Cr (III) led to little fluorescence enhancement because recent studies indicate that Cr (III) cannot readily enter the cells, and on the other hand can probably increase plasma membrane permeability of cells. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity decreased in expose of Cr(VI), as results of the inner filter effect of Cr (VI) ions, but not completely. This can be due to the intracellular reduction of Cr(VI) to the intermediate forms, including Cr(III), Cr (IV) and Cr (V) [58–60]. These results established the feasibility of isophthalic acid-doped PDs for the fluorescence imaging analysis of Cr(VI) in cells. Therefore, by considering that Cr(III) is an essential mediator in some cellular functions such as carbohydrate and lipid metabolisms in compared to Cr(VI), this approach can be used for specifying of chromium in cells [60].

In this study, functionalized polymer dots were synthesized through easy, fast, economical, and environmental-friendly nano-coprecipitation of PFBT polymer and IPA to result in carboxylic acid-functionalized PDs. The prepared PDs can be efficiently used for determination of chromium species in a short time of 1 min in water samples because the novel designed probe can selectively interact with Cr(VI) based on IFE arising from overlapping absorption-emission spectra of Cr(VI) and PDs. The strong fluorescence of PDs is selectively and significantly quench with Cr(VI), while it does not change by Cr(III) ion and, thus, can facilitate a chromium speciation process. The proposed mechanism is an inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism, in which the absorption bands of Cr(IV) overlaps with the emission and excitation bands of the modified PDs. It should be emphasized that our prepared fluorescent IPA/PFBT PDs are very stable and last more than 2 months in aqueous. These PDs are not precipitated out from water, and their optical properties (absorption and emission) do not change significantly over the period of 2 months for highly sensitive and selective determination of Cr(VI). Moreover, isophthalic acid-doped PDs has shown facile surface functionalization accompanied by high quantum yield, good photostability, and excellent biocompatibility; thereby it could be successfully used as a fluorescent on-off probe for speciation of Cr(VI) and Cr(III) as well as in imaging and tracking of chromium species in live HeLa cells. Finally, it should be mentioned that a main limitation of this work is that there are few number of molecules like IPA that can be doped to the PFBT PDs, as they must be soluble in THF solvent in a primary step.

Mojtaba Shamsipur, Project administration, Supervision, Writing - review & editing; Mousa Zare-Moghadam: Investigation, Designing and Application, Formal analysis; Fatemeh Molaabasi, Conceptualization, Validation, Writing - review & editing; Behnam Hajipour-Verdom, Cell imaging.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support of this work Iran Science Elites Federation (ISEF) and Research Councils of Tarbiat Modares and Razi Universities is gratefully acknowledged.

Ju. Lurie, Handbook of Analytical Chemistry, MIR PUBLISHERS, Moscow, Table 10 (1975).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Direct and simultaneous determination of copper, chromium, aluminum, and manganese in urine with a multielement graphite furnace atomic absorption spectrometer
